speaker: interviewer
text: Mrs. Breder, tell us a little bit more about hiding in that hole and the friends you had who protected you.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a pure blessing that, you know, we could do that. And it's only one in a million because this commander was only one. And we had absolutely a chance to survive if we could, because of the condition of the food, of-- of the hygiene. It was they had a shower. And only because the SS knew the clothing goes to Germany, they didn't want the lice and the things brought all the way to their [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were kept clean. We are [INAUDIBLE]-- I mean we had the chance to clean ourself. We had soap. We had water. And during the working time, we could steal the food. And we had the chance to look better than that rest of the inmates. That was pure luck. Now, how did the other inmates wash themselves?

speaker: interviewee
text: They didn't. How could they wash when it was no water? The first time what I recall, it was December, 1942. It was the biggest selection in the camp whatever happened. We didn't go to work. And then we had to march, after roll call, march out. Outside in front of the barbed wire was a big, flat place. They called it [NON-ENGLISH]-- you know, a flat, big place.

speaker: interviewer
text: A meadow.

speaker: interviewee
text: A meadow. It was no meadow. It was no-- no nothing-- no greenery, nothing there in December, and bitter cold. So when we were all out, the whole emptied-- this time, we were in B1 Birkenau. It was the camp was overcrowded. And the-- the Nazis, the SS, expected fresh arrivals-- new income to the camp. So they had to get rid of us.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in the evening, we had to go through the gate back to the camp-- [INAUDIBLE] was not evening it was-- late in the afternoon-- one by one, and SS on both sides, and nude. They were inspecting us. And who had only a blemish, it was right and left. On the side where the-- where the-- behind the [NON-ENGLISH] where the barrack was, where the [? all-- ?] administration barrack-- [SIGHS] on one side where the wooden barracks were, one part-- and those who were marked or were [INAUDIBLE] or were exhausted or not only-- didn't look any more like human beings on one side. And the other was who were-- who were to life-- to work, I mean-- slave labor. So at this time, you know, we were there. We were OK. So we went to this side. And we were on the barrack 1,000, including the 300 from our commando. When we came back on the barracks, beside our 300-- we had a special barrack you know, in-- in Birkenau, in the Kanada. Only 20 was left on the barrack. Almost 1,000 were killed. The whole Birkenau B1 was exterminated.

speaker: interviewee
text: The girls, some of them, were pretty good, screaming and [INAUDIBLE]. Right away, they were unloaded to the trucks and taken to the gas chamber. That was the biggest selection which I was in Auschwitz. You came back, the camp empty. And it didn't take long. It filled up with new arrivals from Europe. Then they moved the men from [INAUDIBLE] B to another camp and started filling up women in-- in Birkenau-- but it was another section, again.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Children never came to-- to the camp. Although, they came-- you know when? In 1944, which we'll get to it, when the Hungarian transports came-- when he started-- Mengele-- collecting the twins. And after the uprising, they didn't gas any more. So they came. And the family [INAUDIBLE] from Terezin in 1944-- I think so. Yeah. In 1944-- came from Terezin family transport, and they kept the whole families together. I don't know if it's-- you know about it. I'm-- it's in the history books. The family transport, in 1944, when it came from Terezin, they kept them all. Because before, it was an inspection in Auschwitz from the Swiss Red Cross. But nothing Birkenau was. They didn't bring them to Birkenau. In Auschwitz. But for this time, they made one barrack, which they put in white sheets. And in Auschwitz, the people looked better, more humane, because they had water. And maybe the stealing and [INAUDIBLE] was not so much like in Birkenau. So they showed them Auschwitz concentration camp, but not Birkenau. And some rumors already came to Switzerland-- if they wanted to know or not. And the Jews-- I don't know from where-- paid some big sum of money that the [? familian ?] transport-- that the families were kept together. So they kept for, I think so, four or five months, the whole families from children and parents. They were together in the barracks. But one night, they exterminate them all, the whole family. And the same they did with the gypsies. The gypsies had a special lager in Birkenau, a special [NON-ENGLISH] I would say. It's called a gypsy camp. Also, they kept them all together. And one night, they exterminate them. They gassed them all.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have no idea. I can't tell you the sum because I [? didn't ?] [? know. ?] And I didn't know-- I-- I don't know even the family in lager. But, you know, we had a very good friend, Erik Kolka, who in 1968, from Prague, went to-- he worked with me. He worked in the book business. I was a manager for 15 years in the largest military bookstore in Bratislava. And he was in Prague. So we met many times. And we got good friends. He was also in Auschwitz a long time. He was in Auschwitz to repair, you know, like a mechanic, a plumber.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we-- he came very often to the working place where I working. So, you know, we exchange a few words. After the war, we met. And since '68, he went to Israel. And he was working at a Hebrew university in Jerusalem. And from Jerusalem, he visited us in-- in America quite a few times. Later on, he got an honorary doctor degree from the-- from the Chicago University. Can we go back to Birkenau and the gypsies? Had you any personal contact with gypsies at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: With the gypsies-- no, I didn't-- because they were always separated, since they start bringing them in. Although, we had, in Birkenau, also lesbians-- I [? can ?] say homosexuals because I was in [INAUDIBLE], which I knew from [INAUDIBLE]. She's still alive and very good friend of mine. Whenever we are in Bratislava, we visit her. She was a physician in Auschwitz too. Dr. [? Muncie ?] [? Schwalbova. ?] And I know one Reichsdeutsche [? politzche ?] with whom she was very good friends. And it was rumors that they were lesbians. Then I know kapos-- the German kapos-- which were also rumors. But I can-- I didn't have-- I mean-- any experience from the Jewish girls which where, you know, close at the-- our work or in the barracks with me, that I know about it. But I know it was rumors that they are homosexuals there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did these lesbian kapos ever hit upon the inmates?

speaker: interviewee
text: Probably they did. I don't know because I had always the same kapo. And working in-- in-- in the unit where I was, she never hit us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Not hit. I mean tried to seduce some of the inmates.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I don't know. In this field, I really don't know if somebody from the Germans was a homosexual [? and hit ?] or she wasn't. But mostly, they were very cruel. So hard to say. Hard to say. [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewer
text: Were most of your kapos women?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: Were most of your kapos women?

speaker: interviewee
text: All kapos were women. And all the SS who guarded us were women SS. Only the blockfuhrer-- like I remember the names of it-- blockfuhrer Towber. He's a-- he was a Viennese sadist. And the-- the kapo-- the blockfuhrers in other-- [? Schtivitz. ?] And then also-- I wouldn't say-- I don't want to use the S-O-B word, but I said it anyway. But those were born sadists or trained sadists. I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe some of those incidents?

speaker: interviewee
text: The sadistic-- they, you know-- if you went through in camp, if you saw even a SS woman-- because they were wearing those-- those whips, leather whips-- you avoided to get close, even for two meters from them. Because if they saw somebody approaching or going behind, just turning her head-- [MAKES WHIP SOUND] The whip went through the head, and then she pulled it. So you avoided. But they were very, very cruel. I don't know one who had a little bit of human feeling. I don't know one. And the men [? was ?] [? ditto-- ?] for instance, those who I was testifying against. And SS-- they were not only Germans in the SS, they were Latvians and they were worst than the Germans. Volksdeutsche, Latvians, Hungarians, Romanians, even from Slovakia. And from all the occupied-- Hitler-occupied, you know, countries where they became later Hitler-oriented, you had the SS. I recognized them on their accent because I speak so many those European languages. If somebody speaks German, like me, you can recognize I have some Slavic accent. So I heard them talking. Either they had the Hungarian or a Russian-- I mean, Ukraine-- the Ukrainians, ha. Those were, you know, SOBs and sadists and killers. You have no idea. Worse than the Germans. Worse. And they hardly spoke in German. But the Ukraine language, some [? are ?] speak like, you know, even I learned in school. You recognize 1, 2, 3, like you recognize the [INAUDIBLE] speaking German-- [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: Speaking English. Do you? So we recognized that. So we know they were from there, but those were killers. I tell you. It's no wonder, you know, they killed before they came to the camps. [NON-ENGLISH] in-- in the Ukraine, in Litva, everywhere, in Poland, in Serbia. Yes, the Bosniaks and the Serbs killed, the mass graves there. And the Pollacks-- you mean they were-- they were gentle? The Polish anti-Semitism is very well known. For the Polish non-Jewish prisoners, you know, political-- in my commando were about 30 Polish inmates-- prisoners. But do you think so they called us different [NON-ENGLISH]? It means Jew, dirty Jew. They still pointed at us there too. We were there the underdogs for everyone, not only for the Germans. I'd like to go back just a little out of curiosity. When you mentioned that the Red Cross from Switzerland came and were shown, in Auschwitz, some model

speaker: interviewee
text: Barrack and--

speaker: interviewer
text: Model barrack--

speaker: interviewee
text: And prisoners in a clean, neat uniform, well-fed. Yes!

speaker: interviewer
text: My question is, why didn't they take some information to the powers-that-are, just the fact that there's a concentration camp, in general?

speaker: interviewee
text: They didn't. It didn't help at all. The same was in Terezin what I know. The Red Cross inspection came, and they clean up. They made the music play. You know how it is. In Auschwitz, you know very well. By now, you heard so many things about Auschwitz. You very well know what went on there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why do you think the Red Cross didn't report the goings-on?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that's a question which I have to study and read because I don't know. I don't know. They didn't tell the world this time that the killings were going on there in the crematorium. And you know what the worst thing was? I told you I was a witness in Vienna and then in Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll talk about that later. Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: The worst thing was then in 1944, when the Hungarian transports came. I was already this time, you know, three years in camp. And, you know, before the Hungarian transports started to come, us-- [INAUDIBLE] where I was working-- they relocated us again. Our working place, until then, was in Auschwitz in the commercial part where they had kind of factories, where they had-- you know, in the meantime, [NON-ENGLISH], [INAUDIBLE] industry-- tens of thousands marched to work to those, you know, German [? munition ?] factories. And we, the Kanada, the location with the clothing, was also in [INAUDIBLE]-- how you say [INAUDIBLE], in Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewer
text: So we had there four barracks, but they were so full of clothing and-- from whole Europe. And they expected hundreds of thousands from Hungary. So the unit whereabouts I working, they relocated us. You know where? Close to the crematorium! Where the trains came in, there were 40 barracks, one lager special for the Kanada. And we lived and worked on the same place, but they added another 300. We were 600 girls there working and men also. But they were less, because the men-- you know what they did? They waited the transport on the ramps. But they brought the luggage when the people came, and they-- they-- on the ramp, they had to line up for the [? sort ?] [INAUDIBLE]. The luggage was piled up. And the luggage, they brought into the camp where we were next to the gas chamber. The gas chambers, crematorium 3 and 4 was so far like across this-- the houses here. It's about 50 feet, yes? Maybe a little more? So the gas chambers were there.

speaker: interviewee
text: The gas chambers, the crematorium, were not surrounded by a wall. It was a brick building, red, nice, nicely landscaped around, green grass. And our, you know, working place in the Kanada-- you know, barracks where the clothing were stored-- were divided from there not by high voltage, only by barbed wire. The first two barracks were our living quarters, dormitories, barrack 1 and 2. And 18 were the working places. On the other side, the men had, I thinks only one barrack. And also, those men were the commanders at the ramp who brought the luggages in that we can work with it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the people, you know, they were standing there, the men, while the [? sort ?] [INAUDIBLE] went on. And from-- when-- in 1944, where we were already in Brzezinka-- this is Birkenau, our working place-- we were separated completely from the rest of the inmate. We didn't have any contact with them any more.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was the [? sauna ?] where the people were undress who came to live, I mean, the working, slave laborer. And 95% went straight to the gas chamber. And you have to look on day and night, seeing the flames high in the skies, and-- and the smell, and the clouds settled, and the ashes-- black, greasy, [? fat ?] on our faces. But I-- we worked 12-hour shifts in the barrack, but my work was later outside. I was sorting with four girls, Lutka from [? Keltze, ?] Reshka from Czechoslovakia, and Adela, and me. [NON-ENGLISH], you know, the pots and pans what people brought out, we had to inspect and sort because there was lots of food.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's see. You know, schmaltz, you know, grease from goose, in big pans. And they were hiding their valuables. So we had to take out everything and, you know-- to search that, you know. But we didn't have to-- we couldn't throw away. Many were glasses were broken. The good stuff, salami, cheeses, you know, durable foods, packages with sweets and so, went on one side. The broken things, which were glass and-- and-- mixed with glass went to the [? lagerkuche, ?] to the lager kitchen, because for us, you know, it's good there too. But they didn't throw it away, you know? You [? chew ?] glass even anyway. So that was my work outside. So I saw the coming and going, everything. That's the reason I could recognize so many SS because I was working outside. And since Kanada had so many goodies, the SS men came-- and you know, the kapos of overall-- they wanted furs. They took jewelry. They were stealing there. So, you know, when you saw so many years the same [NON-ENGLISH]-- in 1943, was already the SS, you know, in-- in the-- our commando. Otto Graff too. Then Gottfried Weiser who I was witness-- a witness in [INAUDIBLE] was there too! So we-- you know, and I have such a good memory. I saw him, and I remembered him. Yes? I remember everything what happened 55 years ago, but I hardly can remember what happened yesterday what I had for lunch. [? You'd ?] remember? I don't, unless it was something good.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway-- so that's the reason, you know, I go and I'm a witness because I promised myself-- first of all, I wanted to live. And I wanted at least to point at one. And it happened. I feel so lucky and proud that I could at least one bring to justice. And it was really something what I-- what I earned and I wanted to do. And I wanted to live just for this reason. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And so what can I say? I will have to tell you now what happened when the Hungarian came. The first transport from Hungary came the 15th of May, 1944. We were already in this special assigned camp where you know, their belongings were stored.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's, you know, you have no idea. In front of us, to the entrance, to the gas chambers, and to the crematorias were lines day and night. The crematoria 4-- 1 2 on one side, the camp. Other side where we were working, the other two. Lined up-- it was hot in this last summer of 1944 when I was still there. And the crowds and the heat and the lines, endless. Endless. You know, you couldn't see the last end. It was very hot, and the people were tired. And then, you know, they were screaming in Hungarian-- [HUNGARIAN], [HUNGARIAN]. It means water, water. They saw us walking there, and they thought, you know, look like human beings. We had this time blue, you know, working pants and white shirts.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I have a picture from there I'm going to show you from Auschwitz. And they were saying-- but one of my coworkers couldn't take it. She went and-- because they had water there-- filled up a bottle of water and throw it over the fence. A young child ran after the water. But suddenly, a SS who was guarding there-- you know, they had bayonets on their-- and they went with this-- run after [INAUDIBLE] the child. And he caught the child. He tore out the bottle of water, threw it away. And he tossed the child in the sky and picked it up on his bayonet. And that was not enough. It was very close already to the entrance through the gate, where you-- behind the red brick gate when he went in. And he smashed his head against the wall. Can you imagine us? But that's not all. Then he came into our working place. The whole 600 had to line up, and then asked, who did it? Naturally, nobody said one word. He said, if we are not going to-- to step out, I will shot every 10-- I think so-- of you. Nobody did it. You think so he didn't? He did. I was not the [? 10. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: And this SS was the one who was sent [INAUDIBLE] where I was a witness in Wuppertal, who on my testimony, he was sentenced. But they had, you know, they had-- it was well known. And it was a record of it. So he was sentenced for life in prison. Germany has no death sentence. If they would have, they would sentence him to death. But I will talk about that what he did. But while I am at him, I'm going to tell you what happened with him. He was-- his name was Gottfried Weiss, native of Wuppertal. Sunningen is next city. After the war, he just came back to the city. He was an architect, and he got very rich. In 1962-- I think so-- somebody recognized him and reported. But in the meantime, these men died. But still, they were searching. In 1986, I was called here in San Francisco to the German consulate. And they ask me if I could-- they had an album of SS who were-- who were-- I mean-- who the people looked for. I mean, [INAUDIBLE] [? Clarksfield ?] and the other, [? Simon ?] [? Wiesenthal ?] [? and so-- ?] And-- OK. Do you have to--
